audit_items.proof_trace: The guard at L258 (`if (bv==NULL || bv->bv_len==0)`) does not check `bv->bv_val`; if `bv->bv_val==NULL` with `bv->bv_len>0`, the function calls `ber_put_ostring(ber, NULL, bv->bv_len, tag)` (L262), which then calls `ber_write(ber, NULL, len, 0)` (io.c L121: `AC_MEMCPY(*p, buf=NULL, len)`) — a null pointer dereference. The same unguarded path is reachable from `ber_printf` case 'O' (L568-570), which only tests `bv==NULL` before dispatching. The `assert(buf!=NULL)` inside `ber_write` is debug-only and absent in production builds.
audit_items.attack_scenario: Under memory pressure, `ber_get_stringbv` (LBER_BV_ALLOC) sets `bv->bv_len` from attacker-supplied network data before malloc, then returns LBER_DEFAULT with `bv_val=NULL`; if a caller on this path ignores the error and passes the half-initialized berval to encoding (e.g., during LDAP Add/Modify response construction), `ber_put_berval` crashes the server via a null-source `memcpy`.
